question: Your tee shot lands in a yellow penalty area (water hazard). What are your options?
ground_truth: **Rule 17.1d** - "The player may play the original ball or another ball from where the previous stroke was made" and "keeping the estimated point where the original ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area between the hole and the spot where the ball is dropped" **Ruling**: You have two relief options from a yellow penalty area, each with a one-stroke penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: (1) Stroke-and-distance relief: Play from where your last stroke was made, adding one penalty stroke. (2) Back-on-the-line relief: Drop outside the penalty area keeping the crossing point between the hole and drop spot, adding one penalty stroke. Unlike red penalty areas, lateral relief is not available. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-17
response:
RULING: Since your tee shot came to rest in a yellow penalty area, you have three options under the rule governing penalty areas:

1) You may play your ball as it lies within the penalty area.
2) You may take stroke-and-distance relief by going back to the tee and playing again from there, adding one penalty stroke. 
3) You may take lateral relief, by dropping a ball within two club-lengths of the point where the ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area, adding one penalty stroke.

You must decide on one of these three options before proceeding with your next stroke.

question: You hit your tee shot and it goes out of bounds. What must you do?
ground_truth: **Rule 18** - "the player must take stroke-and-distance relief by adding one penalty stroke and playing the original ball or another ball from where the previous stroke was made" **Ruling**: When your ball goes out of bounds, you must take stroke-and-distance relief. **Penalties/Procedures**: Add one penalty stroke to your score and play another ball from the tee (where the previous stroke was made). A ball is out of bounds only when all of it is outside the boundary edge of the course. The original ball must be abandoned once you put another ball into play. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-18
response:
RULING: If your tee shot goes out of bounds, you must take stroke-and-distance relief by playing your next stroke from where you last played your previous stroke, under penalty of one stroke.

This means you must return to the teeing area and play your next shot, adding one penalty stroke to your score.

question: Your ball is in a greenside bunker. Can you remove a small stone lying next to your ball before playing your shot?
ground_truth: **Rule 12.2a** - "Before playing a ball in a bunker, a player may remove loose impediments under Rule 15.1 and movable obstructions under Rule 15.2" **Ruling**: Yes, you can remove loose impediments like stones from bunkers without penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for removing stones or other loose impediments from bunkers. Incidental contact with sand during removal is permitted. However, you cannot deliberately touch sand to test conditions or during practice swings (Rule 12.2b - general penalty applies). **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-12
response:
RULING: You may remove the small stone in the bunker near your ball, without penalty.

The rule governing loose impediments in bunkers allows you to remove loose impediments, such as stones, provided you do not move your ball or cause it to move. You must be careful when removing the stone to ensure your ball does not move from its position.

question: Your ball comes to rest on a paved cart path in the fairway. Are you entitled to free relief?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1** - "You are only allowed to take free relief from a cart path if it is artificially surfaced" **Ruling**: Yes, you are entitled to free relief from an artificially surfaced (paved) cart path, which is classified as an immovable obstruction. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. Locate the nearest point where you no longer have interference, drop within one club-length of that point not nearer the hole. The ball must stay in the same area of the course. Natural grass or sand paths do not qualify for relief. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/articles/taking-free-relief-under-the-rules
response:
RULING: Yes, you are entitled to free relief from the paved cart path.

The rule governing relief from abnormal course conditions, such as a paved cart path, allows you to take free relief. You may take relief by dropping the ball within one club-length of the nearest point of complete relief, no nearer the hole.

question: Your ball is on the putting green. Can you repair spike marks in your line of putt?
ground_truth: **Rule 13.1c** - "Damage on the putting green includes shoe damage (such as spike marks) and scrapes or indentations caused by equipment or a flagstick" **Ruling**: Yes, you can repair spike marks on the putting green, including in your line of putt. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for repairing spike marks. Use your hand, foot, body part, ball-mark repair tool, tee, club or similar equipment. Must not unreasonably delay play. Cannot repair natural surface imperfections, aeration holes, or damage from normal maintenance practices. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-13
response:
RULING: Yes, you may repair spike marks or other damage on the putting green that is on your line of putt.

The rules allow a player to repair any damage on the putting green, such as ball marks, hoof marks, or shoe spike indentations, that is on their line of putt. This includes repairing damage that may affect the movement of the ball. You are entitled to repair these types of imperfections on the putting green prior to making your next stroke.

question: In match play, you and your opponent both make a 5 on a hole. What is the result of the hole?
ground_truth: **Rule 3.2a(2)** - "The player and opponent complete the hole in the same number of strokes (including strokes made and penalty strokes)" **Ruling**: The hole is halved (tied). **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. When both players complete a hole with the same score, the hole is halved and neither player wins the hole. Players can also agree to halve a hole after at least one player has made a stroke to begin the hole. The match status remains unchanged for that hole. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-3
response:
RULING: The hole is halved.

When both players make the same score on a hole in match play, the hole is halved, meaning each player wins half a hole. This is the standard result when players tie on a hole in match play.

question: You hit your ball into thick rough and cannot find it. How long do you have to search before it is considered lost?
ground_truth: **Rule 18.2a(1)** - "A ball is lost if not found in three minutes after the player or their caddie begins to search for it" **Ruling**: You have three minutes to search for your ball before it is considered lost. **Penalties/Procedures**: If not found in three minutes, the ball is lost and you must take stroke-and-distance relief by returning to where your previous stroke was made and adding one penalty stroke. If found within three minutes but identity is uncertain, you get reasonable additional time to identify it. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-18
response:
RULING: If you cannot find your ball after searching for 3 minutes, the ball is considered lost.

The rule governing lost balls states that you have 3 minutes to search for your ball before it is deemed lost. If you cannot find the ball within that 3-minute time period, you must take stroke-and-distance relief by returning to the previous spot and playing another ball, adding a penalty stroke.

question: In match play, your putt from the green hits the flagstick that was left in the hole. Is there a penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty if your putt from the green hits the flagstick that was left in the hole in match play.

In match play, the player has the option to have the flagstick removed or left in the hole when making a stroke from the putting green. If the player leaves the flagstick in and the ball hits it, there is no penalty stroke. This is an exception to the general rule that prohibits a ball striking the flagstick when the player makes a stroke from the putting green.

question: In match play, your opponent concedes your next putt. Can you refuse the concession and putt anyway?
response:
RULING: Yes, you can refuse your opponent's concession and choose to putt out.

Under the rules governing match play, a player may concede the opponent's next stroke at any time. However, the player whose stroke has been conceded is not required to accept the concession. They may choose to play out the stroke if they wish.

question: You think your tee shot might be lost in the woods. You announce you will play a provisional ball and hit another. Your original is then found in the fairway. Which ball do you play?
response:
RULING: You play your original ball found in the fairway.

Since your original ball was found within the 3-minute search time, and it is in the fairway, you must continue play with your original ball. The provisional ball you hit is no longer needed and should be abandoned.

question: You take a free drop from an immovable obstruction. You drop the ball from knee height and it bounces and rolls outside the one-club-length relief area. What must you do?
response:
RULING: Since the ball did not come to rest within the one-club-length relief area after the drop, you must re-drop the ball.

The rules governing relief from immovable obstructions require that after taking relief, the ball must come to rest within the relief area. If the ball rolls outside that area after being dropped, you must re-drop the ball in the correct relief area until it comes to rest there.

question: Your ball is unplayable in a bunker. You decide to take back-on-the-line relief. Must you drop the ball inside the bunker or can you drop outside it?
response:
RULING: When your ball is unplayable in a bunker, you may take back-on-the-line relief by dropping the ball outside the bunker, keeping the point where the ball lay directly between the hole and the spot on which the ball is dropped, with no limit to how far back you may go.

The rule governing unplayable ball relief in a bunker allows you to drop a ball outside the bunker under the back-on-the-line option. You are not required to drop the ball back inside the bunker. The key requirements are that the ball must be dropped keeping the original spot between the hole and the drop point, and there is no limit to how far back you may go along that line.

question: Your ball lies in the general area just outside a GUR boundary, but your stance would be inside the GUR. Are you entitled to free relief?
response:
RULING: You are entitled to free relief from the GUR.

The rule governing GUR (Ground Under Repair) states that if your ball lies in the general area outside of GUR, but your stance or swing would be in the GUR, you may take free relief. You can take relief by dropping the ball in the nearest point of complete relief, no nearer the hole, from the GUR. This allows you to avoid having to play your shot from within the GUR.

question: While searching for your ball in the rough, you accidentally kick it. What is the penalty and what must you do?
response:
RULING: If you accidentally kick your ball while searching for it in the rough, there is no penalty. You must replace the ball where it originally came to rest before you kicked it.

The rule governing this situation is the rule on accidental movement of a ball. If a player accidentally moves their ball while searching for it, there is no penalty, and the player must replace the ball on its original spot.

question: In match play, you play out of turn from the tee. Can your opponent make you replay the shot?
response:
RULING: In match play, if you play out of turn from the tee, your opponent has the option to require you to replay the shot.

The rule governing playing out of turn in match play states that if a player plays when it is not their turn to play, their opponent may require the player to cancel the stroke and play in the correct order. This applies to all strokes, including from the teeing area.

So if you play out of turn from the tee, your opponent can choose to make you replay the shot in the proper order. They are not required to do so, but have the option to make you replay if they wish.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. You find your ball sitting on dry land with a playable lie. Are you required to take a penalty drop, or can you play the ball as it lies?
response:
RULING: You may play the ball as it lies in the penalty area without penalty.

The rule governing penalty areas allows you to play the ball as it lies in the penalty area without incurring a penalty stroke. Since your ball is on dry land with a playable lie, you may play it from that position. There is no requirement to take relief and add a penalty stroke.

question: A movable obstruction (a rake) is lying in a bunker. Your ball is not in the bunker but the rake is on your line of play. Can you move the rake?
response:
RULING: Yes, you may move the movable obstruction (the rake) that is interfering with your line of play, even though your ball is not in the bunker.

The rule governing movable obstructions allows you to move any movable obstruction, including a rake, that is interfering with your stance or area of intended swing or your line of play. This is allowed regardless of whether your ball is in the bunker or not. You may move the obstruction to the nearest point of complete relief, no nearer the hole.

question: You start your round with 14 clubs. On the 5th hole you discover that one of the clubs belongs to another player and was placed in your bag by mistake. How many clubs do you have for the purpose of the 14-club limit?
response:
RULING: You have 13 clubs for the purpose of the 14-club limit.

Since you started with 14 clubs and then discovered that one of them belongs to another player, you have 13 clubs that you have put into play. The rule governing the 14-club limit states that a player must not start a round with more than 14 clubs. As you no longer have possession of the 14th club that was not yours, you are now in compliance with the 14-club limit.

question: In match play, you realize on the 4th tee that you have been playing with 15 clubs since the start of the round. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: The penalty for having 15 clubs in your bag during a round in match play is the loss of hole where the excess club was first used.

The Rules of Golf allow a player to have a maximum of 14 clubs during a round. Using more than 14 clubs is a breach of the rules. In match play, the penalty for this breach is the loss of the hole where the excess club was first used. If the excess club was used during the play of multiple holes, the penalty applies to the first of those holes.

You should immediately proceed to the 5th hole and continue play, having removed the excess club from your bag.

question: Your ball is embedded in the face of a bunker, above the sand, in a grassy area that is part of the bunker wall. Are you entitled to free relief for an embedded ball?
response:
RULING: You are entitled to free relief for an embedded ball in this situation.

The rule governing embedded balls states that free relief is allowed when your ball is embedded in its own pitch-mark in any part of the course, except in a penalty area. Since this lie is in the grassy area of the bunker wall, which is part of the general area, the embedded ball rule applies and you may take free relief.

To take relief, you should mark the spot, lift the ball, and drop it within one club-length of the spot where it originally lay, not nearer the hole, in the general area. The ball must be dropped in a way that it first strikes a part of the course in the general area.

question: You play a provisional ball from the tee. Your provisional lands in a red penalty area. Before searching for your original, you play a second provisional from the tee. Is the second provisional valid?
response:
RULING: The second provisional ball is not valid.

Once you have played a provisional ball, the Rules do not allow you to play another provisional ball for the same shot. If your original ball is then found outside the penalty area, you must continue with the first provisional ball you played. If the original ball is found in the penalty area, you must take relief under the applicable rule governing penalty areas.

question: Your ball is at rest on the putting green. A strong gust of wind moves it into a bunker. Must you play it from the bunker or can you replace it on the green?
response:
RULING: You may replace the ball on the putting green without penalty.

The rule governing balls moved by wind on the putting green allows you to replace the ball on its original spot, even if it has moved into a bunker. Since the wind alone caused the ball to move, there is no penalty, and you are permitted to put the ball back where it was originally at rest on the putting green.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. You decide to take lateral relief, but the nearest point within two club-lengths is on the other side of the penalty area. Can you drop there?
response:
RULING: No, you cannot drop on the other side of the penalty area when taking lateral relief.

When your ball is in a red penalty area, the rule governing penalty areas allows you to take lateral relief by dropping a ball within two club-lengths of the nearest point of complete relief, not nearer the hole. However, that relief area must be on the same side of the penalty area as your original ball. You are not allowed to go to the other side of the penalty area to find your nearest point of relief.

To proceed correctly, you must find the nearest point of complete relief on the same side of the penalty area as your original ball's position, and drop within two club-lengths of that point, not nearer the hole.

question: In match play, you and your opponent both play wrong balls on a par 3. You each realize the mistake before teeing off on the next hole. What is the ruling?
response:
RULING: Since you and your opponent both played the wrong ball on the previous hole, the correct ruling is that the hole must be replayed with the proper balls.

The rule governing playing the wrong ball states that if a player makes this mistake and realizes it before teeing off on the next hole, the player must correct the error by playing the correct ball. In match play, since you both made the same mistake, the entire hole must be replayed from the teeing area using your correct balls.

No penalties are applied in this situation, as long as you both realize the error before starting the next hole. You and your opponent should return to the teeing area of the previous hole and play it over using your proper balls.

question: Your ball is in the general area and an immovable obstruction (a sprinkler head) interferes with your stance. However, the obstruction is inside a red penalty area while your ball is outside it. Can you take free relief from the obstruction?
response:
RULING: No, you cannot take free relief from the immovable obstruction (sprinkler head) in this situation.

The rule governing interference from an immovable obstruction requires the obstruction to be in the general area in order to be eligible for free relief. Since the obstruction is located inside the red penalty area while your ball is outside the penalty area, the conditions for free relief are not met. The ball must be in the general area for you to be able to take free relief from an obstruction.

In this case, since your ball is outside the penalty area and the obstruction is inside the penalty area, the only option would be to take relief under the rules for a ball in a penalty area. You would need to either:

1) Drop the ball in the penalty area under penalty of one stroke, or 
2) Take back-on-the-line relief outside the penalty area under penalty of one stroke.

The rules do not allow for free relief from an obstruction that is located inside a penalty area when your ball is outside the penalty area.

question: Your ball is plugged in the steep face of a bunker. You declare it unplayable and choose lateral relief (two club-lengths). All points within two club-lengths that are not nearer the hole are outside the bunker in the general area. Where must you drop?
response:
RULING: Since your ball is plugged in the steep face of a bunker, you may choose lateral relief under the unplayable ball rule. When taking lateral relief, you must drop the ball in the general area, not nearer the hole, within two club-lengths of the spot where the ball lay.

In this case, all points within two club-lengths that are not nearer the hole are outside the bunker, so you must drop the ball in the general area, outside the bunker, within two club-lengths of the spot where the ball was at rest.

question: In stroke play, you putt from on the green and your ball is still moving when it hits another player's ball that was at rest on the green. What happens?
response:
RULING: If a player's ball in motion hits another player's ball at rest on the putting green, the player whose ball was struck must replace their ball on the original spot and there is no penalty to either player.

The rule governing this situation is the rule for balls at rest that are moved by another ball in motion. When a ball in motion comes to rest on the putting green and then is struck by another ball in motion, the player whose ball was at rest must replace their ball on the original spot with no penalty. The player whose ball was in motion also incurs no penalty.

This rule is in place to avoid any unintended movement of a ball at rest on the putting green. The focus is on restoring the original conditions before the incident occurred.

question: In match play, you are 3 down with 3 holes to play. On the 16th tee, your opponent concedes the hole. On the 17th, you win the hole. You are now 1 down with 1 to play. On the 18th green, your opponent concedes your birdie putt, tying the match. Your opponent then says they want to withdraw the concession on hole 16 because they made it by mistake. Can they?
response:
RULING: The concession on hole 16 stands. Your opponent cannot withdraw that concession.

In match play, a concession is irrevocable once made. Once your opponent conceded the 16th hole to you, that result stands and cannot be changed, even if they later claim it was a mistake. The Rules of Golf are clear that a concession, once made, cannot be withdrawn.

The match is now tied going into the final hole, and your opponent cannot retroactively undo the earlier concession they made on hole 16.

question: Your ball is in the general area and you have interference from an animal hole. The nearest point of complete relief would be on a putting green (not the green of the hole being played). Where do you take relief?
response:
RULING: You must take relief from the animal hole, but you cannot take relief onto the putting green as that would be a violation of the rule governing relief from an abnormal course condition in the general area.

The rule governing relief from abnormal course conditions, including animal holes, in the general area requires you to take relief at the nearest point of complete relief, which cannot be on a putting green. You must find the nearest point of complete relief that is not on a putting green and drop the ball within one club-length of that point, no nearer the hole.

question: In match play, you hit your approach shot and it lands on the green. Your opponent, who hasn't played yet, picks up your ball thinking it's theirs. They realize the mistake and replace it. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty in this situation.

The rule governing a player picking up another player's ball is the rule on lifting a wrong ball. This rule states that if a player lifts a ball that is not their own, there is no penalty as long as the ball is replaced on its original spot. Since your opponent realized their mistake and properly replaced your ball, there is no penalty applied.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. Before playing your next stroke, you ground your club in the penalty area and take a practice swing that touches the ground. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: There is a one-stroke penalty for touching the ground in the penalty area with your club during a practice swing.

The rule governing touching things in penalty areas during the preparation to make a stroke states that a player must not touch the ground in the penalty area with their club before making their stroke. Doing so results in a one-stroke penalty. Since you took a practice swing that touched the ground in the penalty area, that incurs a one-stroke penalty.

question: You take relief from a cart path. You determine the nearest point of complete relief and drop within one club-length. The ball comes to rest in a position where you now have interference from a sprinkler head. Must you play it as it lies, or can you take additional relief from the sprinkler head?
response:
RULING: You are entitled to additional relief from the sprinkler head interference, since the original relief drop did not provide complete relief from all interference.

The rules allow you to take relief from abnormal course conditions, which includes obstructions like a sprinkler head. Since your ball came to rest in a position where you now have interference from the sprinkler head after taking relief from the cart path, you may determine the nearest point of complete relief from the sprinkler head and take an additional relief drop within one club-length of that spot, no nearer the hole. This allows you to get complete relief from both the cart path and the sprinkler head interference.

question: In match play, on the final hole, both players are on the green. Player A is 20 feet away, Player B is 6 feet away. Player A putts first and misses, leaving a 2-foot putt. Player B then concedes Player A's next putt. Player A picks up their ball. Player B then three-putts from 6 feet. Player A claims they win the hole because Player B made 5 and their conceded putt means they made 4. Player B says the hole was halved because the concession happened before B finished. Who is correct?
response:
RULING: The hole was halved.

Player B's concession of Player A's next putt took effect immediately, meaning Player A was deemed to have holed out with their previous stroke, even though they had not actually completed the putt. Therefore, Player A's score for the hole was 4, and Player B's score was also 4, resulting in a halved hole.

The rules governing concessions in match play state that a concession takes effect immediately when made, so the hole is considered complete once a player concedes the next stroke. Player B's subsequent three-putt does not change the outcome, as the match was already dormie (tied) after the concession.

question: Your ball is in a bunker. Before your stroke, you smooth footprints in the bunker that are 10 feet away from your ball and not on your line of play. You do this to care for the course. Is there a penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty for smoothing footprints in the bunker that are not on your line of play or within reasonable distance of your ball.

The rules allow players to smooth sand in a bunker before making a stroke, as long as it is not for the purpose of affecting the conditions intended to affect the movement of the ball. Smoothing footprints that are 10 feet away from your ball and not on your line of play is considered caring for the course, which is permitted under the rules. As long as you do not improve the area of your intended stance or swing, or the area of your intended line of play, there is no penalty.

question: In stroke play, you hit your tee shot into the woods. You don't play a provisional. After searching for 2 minutes, your playing partner finds a ball. You identify it as yours but it's in an unplayable lie. You declare it unplayable and take lateral relief, dropping within two club-lengths. After you play your next shot, you discover the ball you found was actually not yours (the markings were similar). What is the ruling?
response:
RULING: You incurred a one-stroke penalty for playing from a wrong ball.

Since the ball you found and played from was not your original ball, you were playing from a wrong ball when you took lateral relief and made your next stroke. The rule governing wrong ball requires you to correct the mistake by continuing play with your original ball, or by taking stroke-and-distance relief.

You must go back and play your original ball from where it was last played, under penalty of one stroke. Any strokes made after playing from the wrong ball do not count.

question: In match play, your opponent's tee shot goes into a red penalty area. They drop under penalty of one stroke and hit their third shot onto the green. You then notice that when they dropped, they dropped on the wrong side of the penalty area (the side closer to the hole). You say nothing until after they two-putt for a 5. You then raise the issue. Is your opponent's score valid?
response:
RULING: Your opponent's score of 5 is not valid. The player is required to take relief from the penalty area on the correct side, and their failure to do so results in a loss of hole in match play.

The rule governing penalty areas states that when taking relief, the player must drop the ball on the correct side of the penalty area, not the side closer to the hole. By dropping on the wrong side, the player has played from a wrong place, which results in the loss of the hole in match play. You were correct to raise this issue after the hole was completed, as it is your responsibility as the opponent to make sure the rules are being followed.

question: Your ball is on a steep slope in the general area. You carefully place your club behind the ball to address it, and the ball moves. Under the current Rules of Golf (2023), are you deemed to have caused the ball to move?
response:
RULING: Yes, you are deemed to have caused the ball to move under the current Rules of Golf.

The rule governing this scenario is the rule on "Ball Moved by Player" (Rule 9.4). This rule states that a player is deemed to have caused their ball to move if the player's actions, including placing a club behind the ball to address it, directly cause the ball to move.

Since you placed your club behind the ball to address it, and that action directly resulted in the ball moving, you are considered to have caused the ball to move. The appropriate procedure is to replace the ball to its original position before it moved, with no penalty.

question: Your ball lands in a flower bed adjacent to the clubhouse. The flower bed is not marked as GUR and there is no local rule about it. Your ball is playable. A committee member walking by tells you that flower beds are always treated as GUR. Is the committee member correct?
response:
RULING: No, the committee member is not correct. Since the flower bed is not marked as Ground Under Repair (GUR) and there is no local rule in effect, the ball is in play as it lies.

The rules do not automatically treat flower beds as GUR. Unless the Committee has adopted a Local Rule to that effect, the ball must be played as it lies in the flower bed. Without an appropriate Local Rule, you are not entitled to free relief from the flower bed. You must play your ball as it lies, with no option for relief.

question: Your ball is unplayable in a penalty area. Can you declare it unplayable and take unplayable ball relief (back-on-the-line or two club-lengths) under Rule 19?
response:
RULING: Yes, you can declare your ball unplayable and take unplayable ball relief under the rule governing penalty areas.

When your ball is in a penalty area, you have several options for relief, including declaring the ball unplayable and taking unplayable ball relief. This allows you to take relief outside the penalty area by dropping a ball under penalty of one stroke under the back-on-the-line or two club-length relief options. This provides you with an additional relief option beyond the standard penalty area relief procedures.

question: Preferred lies (lift, clean, and place) are in effect via local rule. Your ball is in the first cut of rough just off the fairway. Can you lift, clean, and place your ball under the preferred lies local rule?
response:
RULING: Yes, you may lift, clean, and place your ball under the preferred lies local rule since your ball is in the first cut of rough just off the fairway.

The preferred lies local rule allows players to lift, clean, and place their ball within a specified area (often within one club-length) when the course conditions warrant it. Since your ball is in the first cut of rough just off the fairway, this area would typically be covered under the preferred lies local rule. You may pick up your ball, clean it, and replace it within the allowable area, no nearer the hole.
